description: This figure presents Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of microstructures for a novel Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex low-density steel after different cold-rolling and annealing processes. The top row (a₁-f₁) are phase (PH) maps: cyan regions represent face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite, red regions represent body-centered cubic (BCC) ferrite, with a uniform scale bar of 20 μm. The tested samples include cold-rolled (CR) steel, DP950-1 (annealed at 950℃ for 1 min), DP950-5 (annealed at 950℃ for 5 min), DP1000-1 (annealed at 1000℃ for 1 min), DP1000-5 (annealed at 1000℃ for 5 min), DP1050-1 (annealed at 1050℃ for 1 min) from left to right. The bottom row (a₂-f₂) are grain-reference-orientation-deviation (GROD) maps of austenite: yellow denotes 0°~2° orientation deviation (recrystallized grains), warm tones denote 2°~180° deviation (deformed grains), and dark lines are high-angle grain boundaries (>15° misorientation). Observed trends: CR and 950℃ annealed samples contain massive shear bands and incompletely recrystallized austenite, with the highest ferrite fraction in DP950-5; as annealing temperature rises to 1000℃ and above, austenite fully recrystallizes, ferrite fraction decreases, ferrite bands become thinner, annealing twins appear, and austenite grains grow with longer annealing duration. This figure confirms that annealing temperature and holding time regulate recrystallization behavior, phase composition, and microstructure morphology, laying the microstructure foundation for the excellent combination of strength and ductility of the developed duplex steel.
